data.title: Northern California Intermediate Tax-Exempt Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
For most of the final quarter of 2025, the macroeconomic landscape was shaped as much by what investors couldn't see as by what they could. The U.S. government shutdown—lasting a record 43 days—created a significant information vacuum just as markets sought clarity on growth, inflation, and policy trajectories. Despite the data fog, the global macro backdrop proved “good enough” for risk assets. Global growth held firm, inflation stayed sticky but avoided more disruptive outcomes, and most central banks leaned more accommodative than hawkish. In the U.S., elevated downside labor market risk kept U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) easing in play, leading to rate cuts even as the economy expanded. However, policymaker dissents emerged as the Fed Funds rate approached the Federal Open Market Committee’s neutral estimate. International markets—particularly Japan and parts of Europe—offered broadly supportive signals, though political uncertainty occasionally weighed on European sentiment. Since mid-2024, most major developed market central banks have cut rates by 150-200 basis points (1.50%-2.00%) or more, meaning some could be close to the end of their easing cycle. The Bank of Japan remains on a separate trajectory with a gradual pace of rate hikes from a highly accommodative starting point, including two in 2025 and two more expected in 2026. Overall, the global tariff environment proved less disruptive than earlier fears. U.S. monthly tariff collections rose but remained well below levels implied by announced policies, while statutory rates edged lower as deals and exemptions took hold. Still, investors monitored persistent trade-related risks alongside other potential headwinds, including slower AI investment, labor market softening, bond market volatility tied to inflation or fiscal stress, and ongoing risk of geopolitical shocks.

Financial markets capped a strong 2025 with 4Q gains across both equities and fixed income. Many fixed income segments gained around 1%. The Treasury yield curve steepened as short-end rates dropped following multiple rate cuts in the quarter by the U.S. Federal Reserve. Credit spreads were mostly unchanged in both investment grade and high yield, ending near their lowest levels in 2025. Global equities added a little more than 3% as non-U.S. regions outperformed the U.S. in U.S. dollar terms (despite slight U.S. dollar strengthening). U.S. equity returns were positive (roughly 2.5%).

The U.S. Federal Reserve continued easing, delivering 25-basis-point (0.25%) cuts at each of its two meetings. However, an increase in hawkish dissents and adjustments to the policy statement signaled a higher threshold for future reductions. Even so, ongoing easing contributed to further steepening of the Treasury curve, as front-end yields through five years moved lower. The 2-year yield ended the quarter at 3.47%, down 14 basis points (0.14%). In contrast, longer-term rates remained more resilient, with the 10-year yield up 2 basis points (0.02%) to 4.17%.

The municipal bond market ended the year with solid performance, especially in the second half. Municipals outpaced both U.S. Treasuries and broader bond markets during this period. In the fourth quarter, the Bloomberg Municipal Index returned +1.56%, for a total annual gain of +4.25%. In comparison, the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index saw a +0.90% quarterly return and +6.32% for the year. Key drivers included strong demand and Federal Funds rate cuts.

Longer municipal bonds outperformed shorter maturities, as did BBB and AA rated bonds. Credit spreads stayed steady through Q4 but remained below their 10-year averages. Housing and healthcare sectors outperformed other municipal sectors.

On the supply side, municipal new issuance reached $141.5 billion for the quarter—a decline from the previous quarter, but up 12% from the same period in 2024. For the full year 2025, total issuance climbed to $583.8 billion, a 13% increase over the prior year. This heightened supply was matched by strong demand, shown by nearly $12 billion in fund inflows and $109 billion in principal and coupon reinvestment. Overall fund flows for 2025 totaled $51 billion.

Explanation of Results

The Fund outperformed its benchmark in the quarter. The largest contributor to performance was an overweight to longer duration bonds. An overweight to single A detracted.

data.title: Sixth Street Specialty: Far From The Beginning Of The End
data.description:
Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) is arguably one of the most well-known BDCs out there. Investors, who have spent a bit of time researching the sector and gone beyond just index (BIZD) (PBDC) investing have probably noticed that there is a strong consensus among BDC analysts and investors that TSLX is best of breed players, when it comes to NAV durability and overall return sustainability. The evidence on the valuation front clearly validates that - i.e., TSLX trades at a P/NAV of 1.13x, while the sector average is at P/NAV of 0.81x. Plus, 98% of TSLX's trading days since the IPO have been associated with premium valuations, which is something that we cannot say for "98% of other BDCs".

As a result of this, the main investor base (also according to my observations in the Seeking Alpha platform) for TSLX is naturally tilted towards slightly lower yields, lower returns, but with a critical precondition that all of that (e.g., paying huge premium, while the rest of the space can be scooped up at massive discounts) comes with enhanced downside protection and, importantly, durable current income streams, which are not NAV-funded.

However, since July, 2025, when the overall BDC market started to experience one headwind after another one, TSLX has somehow failed to deliver on its promise of being a more durable and resilient BDC:

This is also what I articulated in one of my previous articles which was published back in February, 2025 that TSLX has become highly susceptible to a synchronized downside risk despite its truly robust fundamentals.

Yet, just as my argument in early 2025 and just what the recent sell-off has been driven by, we are effectively talking here about valuation compression and not deteriorating fundamentals in any shape or form that would increase the risk to TSLX's long-term value-creation prospects or the current base dividend.

Let me flesh out my counterarguments to TSLX-bears that might feel victorious seeing the BDC losing ~20% of its market cap in less than 1-year or noticing how the TSLX experienced a further selloff once the Q4, 2025 earnings deck got released.

Thesis review

All in all, TSLX delivered a solid quarter, but not relative to its premium and some other select BDC peers like, for example, Ares Capital (ARCC). Given that TSLX is the most expensive externally managed BDC, I completely share the market's sentiment and, actually, support the activity of penalizing the share price a bit.

There were two key drivers behind the negative reaction:

  1. The adjusted NII per share declined from $0.53 in Q3, 2025 to $0.52 per share now.
  2. The adjusted NAV per share dropped from $17.11 in Q3, 2025 to $16.97 per share now.

To put it differently, the two most important metrics measuring BDC performance weakened, which provides a completely logical ground for the market to do some form of repricing.

Having said that, I think that in the current market (and BDC) environment, it just makes sense to pay extra for enhanced safety and investment durability.

As I have articulated in several of my previous articles, the sector is facing the perfect storm, where we can observe a fusion between market pessimism and some pockets of weakening fundamentals. The pessimism is what explains drastic discounts and huge downside volatility once any new negative data point comes out. The deteriorating fundamentals manifest themselves through more and more BDC divided cuts, structurally declining NII per share due to lower SOFR and continued spread compression.

The overall setup bodes will to consistent base dividend distribution power and sustainable NAVs. Hence, paying extra (basically like an insurance premium) through TSLX's premium I something that I am more than willing to do as that can help greatly mitigate the dividend and NAV risks.

But of course, the big question is whether TSLX still has what it takes to provide the necessary protection.

And here I have a couple of powerful messages.

First, based on the Q4, 2025 NII per share level, the base dividend coverage stands at 113%, which is a) way better than for peers, which have an average of 100%; and b) a very strong margin of safety in the context of 9.5% base dividend yield.

Second, TSLX ended Q4 with a debt to equity of 1.1x, which still leaves about 0.15x of incremental leverage capacity in the system. From this kind of base, TSLX has a room for further portfolio expansion, which can offset forthcoming pressures to NII from lower SOFR, which materialized in the recent quarter.

Most other BDCs are already operating with almost fully exhausted balance sheets, which is another compliment for TSLX and driving factor for the premium.

Third, TSLX has all of its debt base tied to floating rates, which means that the BDC has maximized the organic offset to falling baser rates. There are some BDCs with similar structures, but again, the overwhelming majority has still some fixed rate notes that carry a cost of debt, which is below market levels, introducing an additional headwind on the horizon (i.e., rolling debt into more expensive facility or unsecured note).

Fourth, and this is the most important point, when it comes to credit risk mitigation and portfolio quality, TSLX has functioned like always, which might not seem so on the surface.

The fact that NAV per share has declined goes directly against that argument. However, if we peel back the onion a bit, the key reasons behind NAV contraction was the continued spread compression (accounting for $0.03 per share of NAV drop), slight reversals from the previous gains and lower market price on a public equity name that sits in TSLX's portfolio. None of these dynamics have triggered non-accruals or resulted in failed debt service. In fact, the total non-accrual statistic remained unchanged - at 0.6% of total portfolio fair value.

The bottom line

I am sympathetic of market's decision to derate TSLX by pushing its P/NAV from almost 1.5x in July, 2025 closer to 1.1x now.

There are two reasons that I like about this kind of move:

  1. We can now complement our BDC portfolio with a high-quality instrument at much cheaper price and at lower risk of experiencing price-driven headwinds on go forward basis.
  2. Lower multiple has also increased the base yield to 9.5%, which is very attractive considering the resiliency of underlying fundamentals.

But as I outlined above, the penalty to TSLX has indeed been correctly substantiated by lower NII and NAV per share. More importantly, I think that it also makes sense that TSLX trades lower in the current market circumstances that are net-negative for BDCs.

However, when it comes to the embedded NAV sustainability and base dividend durability, TSLX's offering, which is still there, has arguably become even more attractive (timely).

Finally, I would like to emphasize it again (exactly what I articulated in the previous TSLX-specific article): just as insurance premiums or put options in the financial markets, most of them expire worthless and do not hit the money, which leaves those, who have not paid for the protection better off. Yet, I hope that you will agree with me that this kind of strategy works until it doesn't, and in the prevailing macro environment the odds for risk materialization (e.g., more credit default events) have become tangible.
